Turing’s most famous achievement at Bletchley Park was to develop the concepts behind the ‘Bombe’ machines.
These were electro-mechanical devices used to help find the daily key settings of the Enigma cipher machines, used by the German armed forces to encode their radio messages.

Turing worked on the first computer at Manchester University After World War Two in 1945, Turing joined the National Physical Laboratory in Teddington, London, where he helped to design one of the first electronic computers, known as ‘ACE’.
The Manchester group produced another computer, known as the ‘Mark 1’, which became operational in March 1949.

Turing died young Alan Turing died of cyanide poisoning at his home in Wilmslow in June 1954.

Within weeks of arriving at Bletchley Park, Turing had specified an electromechanical machine called the bombe, which could break Enigma more effectively than the Polish bomba kryptologiczna, from which its name was derived.
The bombe, with an enhancement suggested by mathematician Gordon Welchman, became one of the primary tools, and the major automated one, used to attack Enigma-enciphered messages.
Building on the work of the Poles, they had set up a good working system for decrypting Enigma signals, but their limited staff and bombes meant they could not translate all the signals.

An important step in Turing’s argument about the Entscheidungsproblem was the claim, now called the Church-Turing thesis, that everything humanly computable can also be computed by the universal Turing machine.

In a review of Turing’s work, Church acknowledged the superiority of Turing’s formulation of the thesis over his own (which made no reference to computing machinery), saying that the concept of computability by a Turing machine “has the advantage of making the identification with effectiveness…evident immediately.”
